name: Western Sahara
tagline: A vast Atlantic desert and the homeland of the nomadic Sahrawi, whose colonial past under Spain gave way to a contested, still-unresolved question of self-determination that the United Nations calls the world's longest-pending decolonisation case.
summary: Western Sahara is a sparsely populated stretch of Atlantic desert long inhabited by the Sahrawi, Hassaniya-speaking peoples of mixed Arab-Berber heritage whose nomadic, tribal society was bound to the trans-Saharan caravan trade. Spain claimed the coast in 1884 in the wake of the Berlin Conference and ruled the territory as Spanish Sahara into the 1970s. In 1975 the International Court of Justice found that the territory had not been terra nullius and that, while certain tribes had given allegiance to the Moroccan sultan, no ties of territorial sovereignty bound it to Morocco or Mauritania, affirming the population's right to self-determination. That same year Morocco's Green March and the Madrid Accords led Spain to withdraw and partition the administration between Morocco and Mauritania, triggering a war with the Polisario Front, which had proclaimed the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic (SADR) in February 1976. A UN-brokered ceasefire in 1991 established the MINURSO mission and promised a referendum that has never been held; today Morocco controls roughly the western four-fifths of the territory behind a long sand wall and proposes autonomy, the Polisario controls the area to the east and runs refugee camps near Tindouf in Algeria, and the UN still lists Western Sahara as a Non-Self-Governing Territory.

founding.label: Disputed status / self-determination unresolved
founding.detail: Western Sahara has no internationally recognised sovereign and remains, in the United Nations' own description, the longest-pending case on its decolonisation agenda. Spain, the administering power since the territory was placed on the UN list of Non-Self-Governing Territories in 1963, withdrew in late 1975 under the Madrid Accords without a referendum, informing the Secretary-General on 26 February 1976 that it considered itself thenceforth exempt from international responsibility for the territory. The day after, on 27 February 1976, the Polisario Front proclaimed the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic (SADR). Statehood is contested and unresolved: the SADR is a member of the African Union and is recognised by a number of states, yet it is not a member of the United Nations, while Morocco administers most of the territory and claims sovereignty over it. The UN continues to classify Western Sahara as a Non-Self-Governing Territory whose people are entitled to self-determination, a status that has endured for half a century without a final settlement.

quickFacts.label: UN status
quickFacts.value: Non-Self-Governing Territory (on the UN decolonisation list since 1963); no recognised sovereign

quickFacts.label: Indigenous people
quickFacts.value: Sahrawi — Hassaniya-speaking peoples of mixed Arab-Berber heritage

quickFacts.label: Colonial period
quickFacts.value: Spanish Sahara, c. 1884–1975 (Spain the administering power)

quickFacts.label: Competing administrations
quickFacts.value: Morocco controls ~80% west of the berm; Polisario/SADR control the area to the east

quickFacts.label: Sand wall (berm)
quickFacts.value: Moroccan-built defensive sand wall about 2,700 km long

quickFacts.label: Refugees
quickFacts.value: About 173,000+ Sahrawi in camps near Tindouf, Algeria (UNHCR planning figures)

eras.title: The Sahrawi and the Desert Trade
eras.standfirst: Before any colonial border was drawn, the western Sahara was the homeland of nomadic Hassaniya-speaking tribes of mixed Arab-Berber descent, whose tents, herds, and caravans moved across a landscape with no fixed frontiers.
body: The indigenous inhabitants of the region are the Sahrawi, nomadic Bedouin peoples of mixed Arab and Berber heritage who speak Hassaniya, a dialect of Arabic. Many Sahrawi tribes trace descent from the Beni Hassan, Arab groups who moved into the western Sahara in the medieval period and gradually Arabised the older Sanhaja Berber population in language and culture. Society was organised by tribe and clan rather than by territorial state, and political authority rested with tribal chiefs and assemblies whose leadership shifted with circumstance.

Life followed the rhythm of the desert. Pastoral nomads herded camels, goats, and sheep across seasonal pastures, while the territory's position between the Maghreb and West Africa drew it into the trans-Saharan caravan trade that for centuries carried salt, goods, and other commerce between the Mediterranean world and the Sahel. The absence of fixed borders and the mobility of the tribes meant that allegiances were personal and religious rather than fixed to a defined state.

Encyclopaedic and academic accounts record that the region's tribes resisted external attempts at conquest for long periods, and that when the International Court of Justice later examined the territory's pre-colonial condition it found it to have been inhabited by socially and politically organised peoples — not a land belonging to no one. This pre-colonial society of nomads and traders is the historical foundation on which all later claims and counter-claims would be argued.

events.title: Arabisation of the western Sahara
events.summary: Arab groups associated with the Beni Hassan migrated into the region over the medieval centuries, and Hassaniya Arabic and Bedouin custom gradually overlaid the older Sanhaja Berber population.

events.title: Caravan trade across the desert
events.summary: The territory's position between the Maghreb and the Sahel tied its nomadic tribes to the trans-Saharan caravan trade in salt, goods, and commerce.

events.title: A stateless society of nomadic tribes
events.summary: Authority rested with tribal chiefs and assemblies rather than a territorial state, with personal and religious allegiances rather than fixed borders — a condition the ICJ later cited in finding the land was not terra nullius.

pullquote.text: The Court found that Western Sahara was not, at the time of colonisation, a territory belonging to no one, being inhabited by peoples that were socially and politically organised in tribes.
pullquote.attribution: Paraphrasing the ICJ Advisory Opinion, 16 October 1975

eras.title: Spanish Sahara
eras.standfirst: Following the Berlin Conference, Spain claimed the coast and over the next ninety years consolidated its hold, organising the territory as a Spanish province and later opening one of the world's richest phosphate deposits at Bou Craa.
body: Spain asserted control over the Atlantic Saharan coast in 1884, in the era of the Berlin Conference that regulated the European partition of Africa, and the territory became a Spanish colony. Spanish authority was at first confined to coastal posts, and effective control of the interior came only gradually; in 1934 Spanish and French forces acted to suppress remaining Sahrawi resistance in the region. In 1958 Spain combined the northern district of Saguia el-Hamra with Río de Oro to the south to form the single province of Spanish Sahara.

Under Spanish rule the territory was drawn into a colonial economy. The discovery and exploitation of vast phosphate deposits at Bou Craa, developed in the 1960s and connected to the coast by what is reported to be the world's longest conveyor belt, gave the colony a significant economic resource and heightened its strategic value. Spanish administration also brought the first census and the institutions through which the territory's later status would be argued.

In 1963 Western Sahara, then administered by Spain, was placed on the United Nations list of Non-Self-Governing Territories, making decolonisation and the self-determination of its people a matter of UN concern. As anticolonial pressure grew across the region, a Sahrawi nationalist movement took shape in the early 1970s, and the question of the territory's future increasingly involved not only Spain but neighbouring Morocco and Mauritania, both of which advanced claims to it.

events.title: Spain claims the Saharan coast
events.summary: In the era of the Berlin Conference, Spain asserted control over the Atlantic Saharan coast, beginning colonial rule over what became Spanish Sahara.

events.title: Spanish Sahara province formed
events.summary: Spain united the northern district of Saguia el-Hamra with Río de Oro to the south to form the single province of Spanish Sahara.

events.title: Placed on the UN decolonisation list
events.summary: The UN added Western Sahara to its list of Non-Self-Governing Territories, with Spain as the administering power, putting the self-determination of its people on the international agenda.

events.title: Phosphate mining at Bou Craa
events.summary: Spain developed the rich phosphate deposit at Bou Craa, linked to the coast by an exceptionally long conveyor belt, giving the colony a major economic resource.

eras.title: The Court, the March, and the Withdrawal
eras.standfirst: In a single dramatic year the World Court weighed the competing claims, Morocco marched 350,000 people south, Spain signed away its colony, and a Sahrawi movement proclaimed a republic — setting the terms of the dispute that endures.
body: On 10 May 1973 Sahrawi activists founded the Popular Front for the Liberation of Saguia el-Hamra and Río de Oro — the Polisario Front — to fight for independence, at first against Spain. As Spain prepared to decolonise, Morocco and Mauritania pressed rival claims, and the UN General Assembly asked the International Court of Justice for an advisory opinion. In its opinion of 16 October 1975 the Court found that Western Sahara had not been terra nullius and that, although certain tribes had shown ties of allegiance to the sultan of Morocco and there were some legal ties to the Mauritanian entity, the material did not establish any tie of territorial sovereignty by either over the territory. The Court concluded that nothing affected the application of the principle of self-determination through the free and genuine expression of the will of the territory's peoples.

Within weeks the dispute moved from the courtroom to the desert. On 6 November 1975 Morocco launched the Green March, sending some 350,000 unarmed Moroccan civilians across the border to assert its claim. On 14 November 1975 Spain, Morocco, and Mauritania signed the Madrid Accords (the Declaration of Principles on Western Sahara), under which Spain agreed to withdraw and to install a temporary joint administration with Morocco and Mauritania. Spain completed its withdrawal in early 1976, and in practice Morocco took the northern portion of the territory and Mauritania the south.

The Sahrawi nationalist response was to declare statehood. On 26 February 1976 Spain notified the UN Secretary-General that it considered its presence in the territory ended; the next day, 27 February 1976, the Polisario Front proclaimed the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic. Many Sahrawi fled the advancing armies toward Algeria, where refugee camps formed near Tindouf, while the Polisario, backed by Algeria, prepared for a war it would wage against both Morocco and Mauritania.

events.title: Polisario Front founded
events.summary: Sahrawi activists established the Polisario Front to seek independence, initially fighting Spanish rule; El-Ouali Mustapha Sayed became an early leader.

events.title: ICJ Advisory Opinion
events.summary: The International Court of Justice found Western Sahara was not terra nullius and that ties of allegiance existed but no territorial sovereignty by Morocco or Mauritania, affirming the people's right to self-determination.

events.title: The Green March
events.summary: Morocco sent about 350,000 unarmed civilians across the border in the Green March to press its claim to the territory.

events.title: Madrid Accords
events.summary: Spain, Morocco, and Mauritania signed the tripartite Madrid Accords, under which Spain agreed to withdraw and hand the administration to Morocco and Mauritania, without a referendum.

events.title: SADR proclaimed
events.summary: A day after Spain notified the UN that its presence had ended, the Polisario Front proclaimed the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic.

pullquote.text: The materials and information presented to it do not establish any tie of territorial sovereignty between the territory of Western Sahara and the Kingdom of Morocco or the Mauritanian entity.
pullquote.attribution: International Court of Justice, Advisory Opinion of 16 October 1975

eras.title: War, Withdrawal, and the Wall
eras.standfirst: Guerrilla war against two states followed the partition; Mauritania renounced its claim in 1979, Morocco walled off the territory with a vast sand berm, and the fighting ended only with a UN-brokered ceasefire in 1991.
body: From 1975 the Polisario Front fought a guerrilla war against both occupying powers. Mauritania, the weaker of the two and hard-pressed by Polisario attacks, abandoned the conflict: in August 1979 it renounced all claims to its portion of the territory and signed a peace agreement with the Polisario in Algiers. Morocco then moved to assert control over the area Mauritania had vacated, extending the war along a much longer front.

To contain the highly mobile Polisario fighters, Morocco built a defensive barrier of sand and stone — the berm, or Moroccan Wall — in stages through the 1980s. Reported to run for roughly 2,700 kilometres, fortified and heavily mined, the berm enclosed the economically valuable western part of the territory, including the main towns and the Bou Craa phosphate works, while leaving the thinly populated east beyond it. The wall froze the military map and shaped the line that still divides the territory's two administrations.

After some sixteen years of fighting, the parties accepted a UN and Organisation of African Unity settlement plan. A ceasefire took effect in September 1991, and the UN Security Council, by Resolution 690 of 29 April 1991, established the United Nations Mission for the Referendum in Western Sahara (MINURSO) to monitor the truce and organise a referendum in which Sahrawis would choose between independence and integration with Morocco.

events.title: Guerrilla war over Western Sahara
events.summary: The Polisario Front waged armed struggle against Morocco and Mauritania following the 1975 partition, displacing many Sahrawi toward Algeria.

events.title: Mauritania withdraws
events.summary: Pressed by Polisario, Mauritania renounced all claims to its share of the territory and made peace with the Polisario; Morocco then moved to control the area Mauritania left.

events.title: Construction of the berm
events.summary: Morocco built a fortified, mined sand wall of about 2,700 km in stages to contain Polisario forces, enclosing the western part of the territory.

events.title: Ceasefire and MINURSO
events.summary: A UN and OAU settlement brought a ceasefire; Security Council Resolution 690 (29 April 1991) created MINURSO to monitor the truce and prepare a referendum on self-determination.

eras.title: Stalled Referendum and a Frozen Dispute
eras.standfirst: The promised referendum was never held; for three decades a ceasefire held over a divided territory and tens of thousands of refugees, until fighting resumed in 2020 amid shifting diplomatic alignments and rival peace proposals.
body: The referendum at the heart of the 1991 settlement never took place, deadlocked above all by disagreement over who was eligible to vote. MINURSO continued to monitor the ceasefire while the territory remained divided: Morocco administers roughly the western four-fifths, west of the berm, including the major towns and the coast, and the Polisario Front and its SADR administer the area to the east, which it calls the liberated zone. Tens of thousands of Sahrawi continued to live in long-term refugee camps near Tindouf in Algeria, with UNHCR planning figures placing the population at well over 170,000.

Diplomacy hardened around competing proposals. In 2007 Morocco put forward a plan for substantial autonomy under Moroccan sovereignty, while the Polisario and its supporters continued to insist on a referendum that includes the option of independence. In December 2020 the United States announced it would recognise Moroccan sovereignty over Western Sahara in connection with Morocco normalising relations with Israel, and in 2022 Spain shifted to support the Moroccan autonomy plan as a basis for settlement — moves welcomed by Morocco and rejected by the Polisario and Algeria.

The long ceasefire broke down in November 2020. After a confrontation over a road at Guerguerat in the far south, Moroccan forces moved into a buffer area on 13 November 2020 to clear Sahrawi protesters; the Polisario declared the truce over and fighting resumed along the berm. Western Sahara today remains, in the United Nations' formal classification, a Non-Self-Governing Territory with no recognised sovereign — the longest-pending case on the UN decolonisation agenda. The SADR is a member of the African Union and recognised by a number of states, but it is not a member of the United Nations, and the question of self-determination remains unresolved.

events.title: Referendum stalls; ceasefire holds; refugees remain
events.summary: Disputes over voter eligibility blocked the planned referendum, but the ceasefire held for roughly twenty-nine years as MINURSO monitored a divided territory and well over 170,000 Sahrawi remained in camps near Tindouf.

events.title: Morocco's autonomy proposal
events.summary: Morocco proposed substantial autonomy for Western Sahara under Moroccan sovereignty, while the Polisario continued to demand a referendum including independence.

events.title: US recognition of Moroccan sovereignty
events.summary: The United States announced it would recognise Moroccan sovereignty over the territory in connection with Morocco's normalisation of relations with Israel; the move was rejected by the Polisario.

events.title: Ceasefire breaks down at Guerguerat
events.summary: After a standoff at Guerguerat, Moroccan forces entered a buffer area, the Polisario declared the truce ended, and armed clashes resumed after twenty-nine years.

events.title: An unresolved decolonisation case
events.summary: Western Sahara remains a UN Non-Self-Governing Territory with no recognised sovereign; the SADR is an African Union member but not a UN member state, and self-determination is still unsettled.

pullquote.text: The question of Western Sahara is the longest-standing item on the agenda of the United Nations Special Committee on Decolonization.
pullquote.attribution: Characterisation drawn from UN decolonisation materials

figures.name: El-Ouali Mustapha Sayed
figures.role: Co-founder and secretary-general of the Polisario Front
figures.blurb: A leading figure in founding the Polisario Front in 1973 and a driving force behind the proclamation of the SADR, he was killed in June 1976 during a Polisario raid on Mauritania; the exact circumstances of his death remain disputed.

figures.name: Mohamed Abdelaziz
figures.role: Long-serving president of the SADR and Polisario secretary-general
figures.blurb: He led the Polisario Front and served as president of the Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic for some three and a half decades from 1976 until his death in 2016, becoming the enduring public face of the Sahrawi independence movement.

sources.label: Western Sahara — Summary of the Advisory Opinion of 16 October 1975
Western Sahara — The United Nations and Decolonization
sources.publisher: United Nations, Department of Political and Peacebuilding Affairs

sources.label: United Nations Mission for the Referendum in Western Sahara
